# Transcrição: 2.4-2 Country Level Grad Testing and Scaling

- **Fonte:** 2.4-2 Country Level Grad Testing and Scaling
- **Duração:** 09:00
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Hey, this is Deepash and a common question I get is how do you set up your campaigns for multiple countries and also multiple languages? So this example here is of an Econ brand that are running in Europe predominantly at the moment and we've gone about it with the kind of graduation framework in mind, but then also about how we kind of scale up for each of the countries. And there's a few things that we're doing here that I just want to highlight. So the first thing is for this particular client, we do have a page post engagement campaign running and doing some graduation testing, but this is on a more broader worldwide audience. So it's not literally every single country because I don't want to target those countries that I know that are going to give us a poor return, but we're doing selective testing in different countries to see which ones bubble to the top and show us good performance. So that's at the top level in terms of testing. And then for each country then that we find that we can actually find good results and potentially scale up. What I've got here is a split between a testing campaign and a scaling campaign. So this is set up for most of the countries. I'm only showing active at the moment. So if there's no test, it means the test isn't currently running for that particular country. And what I've got is, and I'll show you this as an example. So I've got two campaigns, one for testing, one for scaling. The reason is because of predictability and stability. When it comes to scaling an ad set and getting consistent performance, there's different levels of history that's actually built up. So the first level is the ad, then the ad set, then the campaign, then the account. If you're doing lots and lots of testing, you're getting lots of sporadic results. Some adsets are working. Some adsets are not. Some ads are working. Some are not. You're making lots of constant changes. That builds into the campaign level data. So when you launch a future ad set, if Facebook doesn't have any history of that ad set, it will go back to the campaign and say, right, how does this campaign generally performed? And that, I found, makes an impact on the auction. So that's why I split my testing from my scaling. Now, you could say, if I'm testing and performance is really good, I want to just continue spending on that ad or that ad set. You absolutely can do that. The problem is, when it comes to stability and stability of your performance, it can suffer. So the way I generally set it up is then by country and by language in this particular instance. So this is Germany and Austria. And they're sharing the German language. So both of those countries in this instance have been grouped together. And then within that, I've got my different ad sets that are currently running, my test ad sets. And within each of those are ads that are running. Now, one thing I want to kind of go through is, so these are all my test ad sets. And for this particular Ecom store, we have a good budget for testing. But actually, what we found is, as we're doing testing, we're generating really good

## [03:00 - 06:03]

sales as well. So if you look at the ROAS here of four on this spend, which is about 3,000 pounds or about three and a half, maybe $4,000. So this is 14 days worth of testing. And we're getting a 4x return. Now, there's a few reasons for that. It's a higher ticket item. It's about $200, I think, is the AOV. And also, we've got a good audience and creative and a sales funnel dialed in. So this is a really mature Facebook ads account. So if you've seen some of the other videos, quite often I'm talking about kind of Starter Ecom stores or start a Facebook ads account. And then maybe some kind of mid-level accounts where we're still doing optimization. This is actually a seven, maybe even eight figure soon Ecom store. And this is what a mature account looks like. Well, test budgets are a lot higher. We're testing at higher velocity. We're still doing graduation testing, but with bigger budgets. And actually, we're finding that the graduation testing is actually bringing in great performance in. So that's obviously a good thing. But for me, and this is going back to grad testing, ROAS isn't the be all and end all. Because in some cases, we don't have enough purchases to make that make a relevant call. Now the other thing about this particular testing is when we're running ads, we've got an automation going, which automatically turns off ads once it hits two and a half thousand impressions. Now, if you go back through grad testing, generally I say a thousand impressions, maybe 1500, possibly 2000 impressions is enough. We've gone for two and a half thousand impressions here because the ticket item is higher. It's a $200 ticket item. So therefore I want to reach more people and funnel more people through before we're making conclusions on results. And that's why they've been cut off at two and a half, maybe could even go to three, three and a half thousand impressions. It's not about the budget. We've got the budget to test. It's more about the validity of results. And we've found that two and a half is a really good number for this particular account and this style of AOV. And then we're looking kind of back at the cost per ad to cart and then maybe the cost per initiate checkout. So for example, we know on average we're getting 187. So this is a really good ad. This is a good one. This one wasn't really as good. This was good. This was good. This is good. Now, if you look at this one in particular, then you look at the cost per initiate checkout. And obviously you look at that number and say, actually, this was better than this. The problem is we don't have enough numbers here to statistically make a decision at that level. Sorry, at the initiate checkout. And even at the cost per ad to cart for this particular one, you'd say, actually, it's a bit more difficult to make a call. And then you go back another level. So cost per content view where actually we have better numbers coming through. Then I'm looking at cost per content view here and scanning down. And so again, based on the average, we'd say that this one is good.

## [06:03 - 09:00]

This one is good, et cetera, et cetera. So when you're doing this kind of testing, look for volume of numbers and make a decision where the volume of numbers are. If you want to get more statistical data that you can rely on to look at website at to cart, look at website initiate checkout or even purchase, then you're going to have to pump in a lot more impressions into your testing. And that's really down to how much budget you want to spend and how quickly you want your results. So for us, for example, we want to continually push new ads in and therefore we want velocity and therefore we make a call early on based on performance. And generally this works for us as well. So that has to be something that works for your particular store, for your e-com account, for your Facebook ads setup. But that's kind of how we do testing. And then once we've found a winning ad or if we're doing audience testing a winning ad set, we move it into the scaling campaign and therefore we're able to push more budget through. So here's an example where we've actually graduated it and so far it isn't giving us the performance we want. This is a slightly different product for this brand, but it's not converting as well as the other products. And that's something which we would then continue looking at ads, looking at potentially cycling new ads to test. And you can see that some of them are, a few of them are bringing in sales, a few of them are not and the cost per ad to cart are actually wildly varied at this stage. So if you look at the numbers, they're not, they're not, okay, maybe 15 is slightly more significant, lots of content views. So we're still pushing more money into tests. Actually I would argue that this ad is performing significantly better than the other ads considering the number of content views and how many are progressing through. So looking at this data so far, I would potentially pause out for you ads, continue running with these, see if I can graduate a few more out. But essentially that's the key thing here. So we've got graduation testing going on at the top level for different countries. Then we split countries into kind of test and scow, continue doing more testing at the country level. And then we graduate when we're ready to scow and then we're looking for stability. Actually this account is given really, really good kind of stable results. And that's because of the testing method that we put in place, which works at this kind of ticket value. We have more budget to spend for testing and it just enables us to make more considered decisions as we're going through a country by country basis. And you'll see this as well as you test in different countries, you'll see some ads and ad copy resonate better than others. So some targeting, you know, look likes work better in some countries and other countries you have to find interest that work as well. It just enables us to split those accordingly.
